explanation: The input states that the system should transition from VentilationOff (a.k.a. StandbyMode) to PSV (Pressure Support Ventilation) mode only when the GUI initiates a change mode command to PSV. Given the list of atomic propositions, StandbyMode corresponds to 'StandbyMode', and the command to switch to PSV mode is captured by 'PSVModeSelected'. The output LTL ensures that whenever the system is in StandbyMode, the only way it can leave StandbyMode and enter PSVMode is upon 'PSVModeSelected'. While there isn't a direct atomic proposition for 'VentilationOff' as a state or for the actual state transitions, the closest match is to require that if at any time the system is in StandbyMode and 'PSVModeSelected' becomes true, then eventually (at some point after or immediately) 'PSVMode' will hold (system is in PSV mode). This can be written as G((StandbyMode & PSVModeSelected) -> F(PSVMode)). Other LTL properties might try to link transitions more tightly with state machine edges, but with only these atomic propositions, this property best matches the intent.

The input natural language expresses that the transition from VentilationOff to PSV should only occur if the command to switch to PSV mode is received from the GUI. Among the provided atomic propositions, 'StandbyMode' best matches VentilationOff (since StandbyMode is described as the same as VentilationOff), and 'PSVModeSelected' means that the GUI command to switch modes has been given. In LTL, this can be captured by stating that whenever the system moves out of StandbyMode (i.e., StandbyMode becomes false) and into PSVMode (PSVMode becomes true), it must be the case that the command 'PSVModeSelected' has occurred. One way to express this is: it is always the case that if PSVMode becomes true in the next step while StandbyMode is currently true, then PSVModeSelected must hold. Formally: G((StandbyMode & X PSVMode) -> PSVModeSelected). This property requires the trigger (PSVModeSelected) at the moment the transition occurs, capturing the intent of the original requirement.

The property states that if, at any time, the user initiates a mode switch to PSV via the GUI (represented by 'PSVModeSelected'), then eventually the system should transition to PSV mode (represented by 'PSVMode'). This approach captures the natural language requirement that the transition from VentilationOff (which corresponds to PSVMode being false) to PSVMode should occur if the change mode command is received from the GUI. The property does not use PSVModeSelected as a trigger for only when in VentilationOff because no atomic proposition directly identifies being in VentilationOff. Thus, the best approximation is to state that whenever the command is received (no matter the origination), eventually PSVMode must follow. Previous properties that might only dictate 'PSVModeSelected -> PSVMode' globally (as we do here) are likely correct unless further information about the origin mode is provided; no better refinement can be specified from these atomic propositions.

The natural language specifies that a transition from VentilationOff (represented by StandbyMode) to PSV (represented by PSVMode) must occur if, at any time, PSVModeSelected (change mode command to PSV is received from the GUI) becomes true. In LTL, this is typically expressed using the 'release' (R) or 'until' (U) patterns. Here, we use 'globally, whenever in VentilationOff, if PSVModeSelected is received, then eventually PSVMode will hold' to capture the required response, as it best matches the semantics available with the given atomic propositions. Note: StandbyMode is described as 'same as VentilationOff Mode', which justifies its use for VentilationOff. Thus, the property states: 'If in StandbyMode and PSVModeSelected is true, then eventually PSVMode becomes true.'

The input natural language specifies that the system should transition from 'VentilationOff' (which matches the atomic proposition 'StandbyMode') to 'PSV' (which matches the atomic proposition 'PSVMode') solely in response to receiving a 'change mode command' from the GUI (represented by the atomic proposition 'PSVModeSelected'). The requirement is that whenever 'PSVModeSelected' occurs (the relevant event), the system must eventually enter the 'PSVMode'. Using LTL, this causality can be captured as: whenever 'PSVModeSelected' happens, eventually 'PSVMode' must hold. This is formalized as G(PSVModeSelected -> F(PSVMode)). Note: There is no atomic proposition corresponding to 'VentilationOff' itself, but the description states 'StandbyMode (same as VentilationOff Mode)', so 'StandbyMode' is used to represent 'VentilationOff'. We do not use explicit state transitions (since there are no propositions for transitions) or numerical comparisons, but instead encode the causality between the GUI command and the target mode.

The natural language requirement specifies that the system should transition from 'VentilationOff' (i.e., StandbyMode) to 'PSV' mode when a change mode command to PSV is received from the GUI. Among the provided atomic propositions, 'StandbyMode' represents 'VentilationOff' and 'PSVModeSelected' represents the change mode command to PSV from the GUI (i.e., switch to PSV mode initiated by the user). Linear temporal logic cannot directly express transitions between states, but we can capture a key invariant: "Whenever the system is in StandbyMode, if PSVModeSelected occurs, the system should eventually enter PSVMode." Since there is no explicit 'PSVModeActive' atomic proposition, we must interpret 'PSVModeSelected' as the explicit signal corresponding to the command. Thus, the best interpretation with the available atomic propositions is: whenever in StandbyMode, if PSVModeSelected is true, then eventually PSVMode will hold, i.e., the system enters Pressure Support Ventilation mode.

The natural language statement requests that the system transitions from VentilationOff (which in atomic propositions is represented by StandbyMode) to PSV when the 'change mode command' to PSV is received from the GUI. The most suitable atomic proposition here for the change request is 'PSVModeSelected' (indicates switch to PSV mode initiated by the user). Thus, the property should state: whenever the system is in StandbyMode (VentilationOff), then if the PSVModeSelected signal occurs, the system must (eventually) enter PSV mode. Since we do not have an explicit 'transition' proposition, but do have atomic propositions about the mode, we capture this as: whenever we are in StandbyMode and PSVModeSelected occurs, then eventually (F) the system will be in PSVMode. This captures the requirement that a change command from the GUI while in StandbyMode leads (eventually) to being in PSVMode.

The natural language statement says that the transition from VentilationOff to PSV mode should occur if the change mode command to PSV is received from the GUI. From the atomic propositions, 'StandbyMode' likely represents 'VentilationOff', 'PSVMode' indicates being in PSV, and 'PSVModeSelected' represents that the command to switch to PSV has been received. To capture the required property, we assert that it is always the case that if the mode selection command is received ('PSVModeSelected'), then eventually the system will be in PSV mode ('PSVMode'). We cannot express explicit state transitions directly, but we can say that receiving the command guarantees eventual transition into the target mode. Thus, the LTL formula: G(PSVModeSelected -> F PSVMode) expresses this required behavior. This is the most direct, simple, and likely translation. There were no previously given LTL properties with this input to compare against.

The requirement is about a state transition from VentilationOff (which, based on the atomic propositions, corresponds to 'StandbyMode') to 'PSV' (Pressure Support Ventilation mode). We are told that the transition will occur if the change mode command is received from the GUI. The atomic proposition that fits this trigger is 'PSVModeSelected', which indicates that the user has initiated the switch to PSV mode. Thus, we want to capture that whenever we are in 'StandbyMode' and 'PSVModeSelected' occurs, eventually 'PSVMode' becomes true. In LTL, this is commonly written as: globally, if (StandbyMode & PSVModeSelected), then eventually PSVMode. This ensures that the occurrence of PSVModeSelected in StandbyMode leads to PSVMode being true some time after.

The natural language states that the system should transition from VentilationOff (StandbyMode) to PSV when the change mode command to PSV is received from the GUI. The closest atomic propositions are PSVModeSelected (indicates switch to PSV mode initiated by the user) and StandbyMode (indicates Standby Mode/VentilationOff). There isn't an atomic proposition for the transition itself, but the intent is to express that whenever the mode change command is received (PSVModeSelected becomes true), the system should eventually (at some time after) not be in StandbyMode (i.e., the system has moved away from VentilationOff). Thus, whenever PSVModeSelected holds, eventually StandbyMode should become false, which is captured as: G(PSVModeSelected -> F(!StandbyMode)). This captures the requirement, as it ensures that every time the PSV mode is selected, the transition from VentilationOff (StandbyMode) will eventually occur.
